speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This second interview with Arthur Sundberg is recorded at his home in Potlatch on July 18th, 1975.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course, it's a pretty well known story, I think. You see, when they come out here, they bought it was already a lumber mill down here in plus a pretty good sized mill. But there timber holdings, I guess, were very limited. They would, you know, buy from individual homesteaders and so on. But they had log drives on this river at that time. I guess they had a lot of water. And in the spring they would drive the logs down to Palouse and some of them to Colfax. They had a mill in Colfax. It was a Tod Lumber Company at that time. But anyway, Potlatch, when they moved out here, of course, they owned this timberland for quite a few years before that. But anyway, they they did figure on building a big sawmill, this big sawmill in Palouse, and turned down that little one they had done there. But the story goes that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That the merchants and the Chamber of Commerce, if they had such a thing at that time, they I guess they got together and said, well, there's a good chance there's a big outfit come in and we're going to make some money off of them. See? Well, old Bill dearie. He was the general manager at that time. He seen just what was up, you know. So he told them. He told his bankers and big shots down there. He said. He said we'll make the grass grow. Clear up to your shoulders right in your main street here in place. And he did too. So they just moved ten miles up the creek and found this little spot down here. And they had to buy out a homesteader down there. And there was a little cabin. I think you probably saw the picture of it in this other book, this little log cabin. Well, that's an actual picture of the cabin that was sitting down there. But anyway, at that time, they.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The river used to follow along the edge of this hill around this way. You know, you know just about where the highway runs now. Well, that was the riverbed at that time. And so they there was a little piece of high ground, you might call it, an island out there. And so that's where they started excavating and started building their sawmill. Then they changed the river channel around to the mill, and then from the mill and on out. Then they had a a lot of low ground then. So they after they started running the running the middle, then they started filling, filling in a lot of this little ground, the old river channel and so on with sawdust and slabs and anything, you know, to fill it up because they wanted to keep that river channel over into the Long Pond area… and… you know, they had a lot of timber and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Those days, you know, it just seemed like everybody thought that they could travel for so many miles, and there was nothing but timber, that there was no end to it. You see, it would last forever. So when they were logged, they would all take it great big high trees and own they'd they'd take 2 or 3 logs off from the bud, end up. And when it got up to where it was, a lot of branches, they'd leave all that. They'd just go off and leave it. They just took the good stuff… well… as I say, they had a lot of this low ground. So from the entrance of the planing mill down there and past the sawmill and, and rounding up through past the depot, and from there up the hill, past the store and bank. And then another branch turned and went up the hill up to this Nob Hill area where all the brass lived. Another one went up the first street in town as Pine Street up to it run about three blocks up to as far as a boarding house. That was all planked. It was a plank road. All of that area was a plank road, four inch by 12 inch plank, 20ft long. So it was about a 20ft wide roadway. So you can imagine how many plank it would take to build all that roadway.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Now that's how cheap lumber was at that time. You see? I can I can still hear them horses of going over them plank roads. And after a while, you know, they the spikes would get loosened up. Some of them plank with the rattle. You know, they went by, you know, you take a team trotting in the wagon, you know, and then planted. Right. Or you could hear them all over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The dairy plan the building of the mill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course, he was the general manager at that time. And of course he… the engineer… that drew the plans was a fellow of the name of Wilkinson. And of course, he was from Minneapolis. And. I don't know whether. Lee Gale that lives he lives out here, just out of town. You probably know him well. He was. The last one to hold a job as an engineer down here at the plant that is the steam engineer. He was a. Head of the power plant and so on. And. So I think, I don't know, I think maybe that he salvaged some of the blueprints, but the blueprints. Oh, they were so badly abused. It was just a crime. But if you could just see the the penmanship on those blueprints, you know, any of the writing and the descriptions and stuff, it was just beautiful. You know, it was a thing that really seemed to see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But… oh, I'd handle those blueprints a good many times myself, you know, looking up details on certain things because. I'd done the engineering and. Do the plans for a lot of the additions and alterations and so on down there for several years. And. I was in charge of the maintenance and construction there for a good many years down at the plant. And. So sometimes we'd have to look up some of these old prints, you know, to get some details off from it. But it is really something to see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were complete and and well drawn drawings.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Well, yeah, there was hundreds of them, you know. You know, they'd be. Schematic drawing of the entire floor plan. And then of course, you had all these detailed drawings, you know, and. Of course, the whole plant wasn't engineered and and built at the, at the beginning. You see, when this plant first started operating, they had four head rigs. Head rigs. That's the carriages. You know, that in the big band saws. You know, that saw the lumber. And they were single cut. In other words, these bandsaw just had teeth on one edge. Nowadays, band saw has teeth on both edges. They cut both directions, you see. But those days it didn't. And then, of course, each Hedwig had a sawyer. He was a man that operated this rig back and forth, and he was the one that that had to make a decision as to what dimension they would cut, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, then there was two men that was riding on this carriage. One was a setter and the other was a dogger. And they would set these dogs, they had these levers, you know, and these dogs had come down into the log and hold the log in place while they were selling it to see. Well, they would cut off a slab, and then the sawyer would, would read. Of course, this had to be done instantaneously. You see, he would read the face of that log after the slab was taken off, see, and determine whether that was a select board or if it was a number one, 2 or 3 four common lumber. See? And he would give the signal by, by finger because it was a noisy you couldn't talk. And they had a lot of finger language in the sawmill. Well, he would signal this to the setter on the carriage what the next cut was to be, whether it was a four quarter board or eight quarter eight board. Of course, that would be two inches he. And those days, and for good many years, they cut an awful lot of pattern material and match stock.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Now, of course, this was all Matt stock and pattern lumber was all cut out of white pine because it was easy work. You see? Well, the. Pattern lumber it matched was generally 3 or 4in in thickness. But anyway, this carriage, it traveled pretty fast up and down the track. You see, it would it wasn't so fast going through the saw, but then going back, he'd just really shoot back there, you see. And then of course this probably he had the index that log ahead. Then you see with had what they call a set works on there, you know. And he had a lever that he could set for whatever dimension he wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was the setter.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Or the setter on the carriage. And this is all steam operated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he took it from the sawyer. What, what?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He got the signal from the sawyer as to what set up. Well, he would set up index at the head then for for 1in or 2 inch or 3 or 4 inch, six inch, whatever the the sawyer called for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then what would the dogger do?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He would just write his dog lever after he dogged it. He just sit down on the lever and he'd just ride back and forth until they went to turn the log. Then, of course, just instantaneously just it had to be synchronized, you know, so there was no lost motion at all. That carries would come back like this, and this wouldn't hardly get stopped until that nigger, the sawyer, operated the nigger with one hand and and the carriage with the other hand. He. And then, of course, he would give these signals with his fingers like this, as opposed to get what he wanted. But he would he would signal for to turn that log, and as he did, why they would release these dogs. This dogger had two levers, one here and one over here. See? And he released those. And of course, he had to ride brace like that, you know, because the thrust of this carriage, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he brace against.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Just on the floor. But boy, he really dug his heels in and and embraced himself. They generally have a bevel cleat there for a little bevel on it to brace against, and they would release these dogs and of course the center. He had one dog he had to release, and then the dogger had two. Well, that nigger would come up by the hole just like that and hit that log and flip it over, and they'd dog it that quick and it was gone again. You know that the whole operation was just just like the battle of an eye. You just had. If you blink twice, you'd miss it. See, it was that fast. Well, anyway, this mill, they had four head rigs. Single cut, and of course, starting in, the logs were down in the water, in the pond, and down there they had a steam drag saw that would these pond men would hold this log up against the boom. They had a platform about four feet wide boom that they walked on, and they would hold that up there and gauge it the right length.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Then they had this steam driven drag saw, you know, it was like this, you know, and they would let it down with a winch, you know, slow. And they'd cut that log off whatever length they wanted before they sent it up into the mill. Well, that's the way they operated then until. In 1910, they put in a 54 saw gang, gang saw. You know, the gang saw is. Tell me. Well, it's just a huge steel and cast iron frame. And in that frame, there's 54 saws that feed in there. And he saw, saw blades were about 4.5ft long. With a tab on each end, you see, and they would fit into a slot and down there and then up here, and then they would they get them all in. Well, then they'd take this hammer and drive these key wedges in to tighten them. Saw as you see these wedges went in. It had raised it up until it tightened them up until they were just like fiddle strings.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you couldn't change the the spacing on those saws. You see whatever they were put in there. Well, that's the way they would be. Well then the head rig, they would saw cants, they would saw these, these Fletchers four inches thick, six inches thick, eight inches, ten inches, 12in thick. The whatever the log would make. And they would flip them over into this area that had chains that carried them over there. Then they had men that stacked these up and made up these loads ahead of time. Well, you can imagine. Then they had these stacked up where they could stack them up 14in high. You know, you could have say a an eight inch camp with a six inch count on top of it. Or you could have a ten inch with a four inch count on top of it. But anyway, they'd make up a load of about 14in high and 54in wide. You know these well, then, of course, they had these power rolls that carried them up, and they come up to this gang saw just before they they come up to the saws that was east always were just really going fast up and down. Well then they had these power rolls, that is rolls that was with a steam cylinder up above that would come down on top of this load and hold them, see otherwise that the load it would jump up and down.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Then these power rolls would carry this load forward through these saws. And of course they had power rules on the other side to hold them as they was coming out of the soil. And as that load cleared, they'd raise these roads and bring the next load in. So they saw a tremendous amount of wood on this gang saw. Well, that wasn't put in until 1910. So, you see, the mill had been operating then for approximately four years before they put in that gang.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did they do before they had the gang saw?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They had to saw it all up on the head rigs. He but they all they would. More than double their production over the four head rigs with the installation of that gang. So is he.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the wood came out of the gang, saw, what, what kind of shape was in? And then it was cut and it was really.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It was all. It was all four squared lumber because the head, the carriage, you know, had taken the slab off and the log on this side turned it over, taking it off on the other side and maybe a couple of cuts. Then they throw the rest of this over. And of course, that would be two sides of it would have probably bark on it, you see. But there's two sides that was flat top and the bottom was flat. Well then as it come out out of the gang there, they had two edges, one on each side. One edger would take this whole load and the next load went up to the next, the other edger. Well, the way they they transported this load over there was that they had some, some arms that come up between these rules and they would come up like this. Well then that loaded would lay on a, on a slant and they just keep breaking it down onto the edger, you know, and, and the boards that didn't need edging, they just sent them through, you know, lap them over and send them through. And then those that needed edging, well, of course he'd have to adjust their saws and edge them to whatever dimension they would make.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Is he. But on the the other edges for the head rigs, it was one man deal. You see, that one man would receive these boards that come in from the head rig and run them through the edger, but on the gang it was two men on each one, because the amount of the volume of lumber coming, you know that this edge, he had to have a helper to break this stuff down and get it through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men ran the gang saw?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it actually there was a the sawyer and then a tail Sawyer, and then one man out in the middle helping stack these camps. So it was only about a three man deal, and they would produce about just about double of what these Hendricks could produce in, in lumber. So, you see, it was really a profitable proposition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Then these double cut saws. They didn't come into into play until. Oh, in the early 30s, you know, after the so-called depression, you know, in the 30s and after that was when these double cut band saws started being used in and they weren't used in all the mills. But then as the mills tried to modernize and and upgrade their, their production, well, then they put in these double cut bands and they could, they could use em on the same equipment that they had, with the exception of just few minor changes, you know, and.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about that, finger language was that used very extensively in the mill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: All over in the mill. They, they would tell stories and they would joke and they would swear and and they do everything, you know, with their fingers, that is fingers and hands, you know, they would you know, they had I never I never got very proficient at it because my work always was where I could talk, you know, I but then when I'd get up in where they were in the area where they were sawn, where there was all this noise, and of course, I would make a few signs if, if, if I want to do to some individual. But it was really fun to watch some of them, you know, that there was characters that worked there, that they carried on a conversation all day long. Just with their hands, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm just trying to feature how you can do that. I guess I can't know because I've never had to work in that, that kind of noisy.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course I'd gotten a lot of it, but of course. That was one. Naturally. That's one and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that? One finger out. And then the thumb up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: At one and a half, you would let it be that it'd be a six quarter. You see that? You know, they they cut a lot of six quarter. And then of course, two, three, four, five, six.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Six is the fist.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Double fist is six. Well then of course this doubled. And then like that that was ten. See that was twice five. See that was ten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh you wiggle your hand.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. And four fingers down would be eight and, and all that stuff. And of course they'd done that so fast and they'd done with both hands, you know, and of course they, they'd rubbed their head and they'd rubbed their belly and they'd do all kinds of things, you know, and if the if there was some joker, you know, it was kidding. Some guy over there about the galley was out with last night. But of course, you know, this would be the girl that was out with, you know, or something like that, you know. Yeah, I had the head. Was fun to watch them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This mean food.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's right. Could mean a lot of things.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's right. To they, you know, they'd rub their belly, you know, that, it was time to eat or something like that, you know, and, and of course, if they was going to sneak a smoke like that, you know that, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two fingers on them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was a there wasn't any smoking allowed on the plant all until… well, it is… about 1926… when they, they built a lunchroom then for the men and they had that steam heated and and of course they could smoke in there. But before that they, of course, they, they'd done a lot of sneaking, you know, they would the fact of the matter is, there wasn't only, well, you might say wasn't any. Modern toilet facilities around the plant take from the time it started until, oh, up until in the. Middle 20s and early 30s. Did they even have water pipe? You know, for drinking water? They had water buckets and dippers all over. You know, everybody drank out of the same dipper, you know, and. For toilet facilities, they just had a building here and another building out there and, you know, with a hole dug in the ground and, and you'd sit on a two before, you know, the. And that's the way, that's the way it was run until.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you might say around between 1925 and 30.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the guys do for eating lunch and taking breaks?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They didn't take no breaks. There was no such thing as a break. You had ten hours work and that was ten hours work. And the only break you got was if you had to go to the John and and if you went there too often, then you got that slip, you went and got your time and you were done. There was no such thing as breaks. And and as far as lunch is concerned, you know, the modern thermos bottle, it didn't come into existence until. Oh, about the time of the First World War, right after the First World War. But up until that time. All the men that carried the lunchbox, it would be just a big tin rectangular bucket with a tray on the top with what looked like a chimney. And this tree held their coffee or whatever they used. And then, of course, this looked like a chimney. That was a cup. You'd pull that off, and then you would pour your liquid out of this thing you see, into this cup.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You probably seen pictures of dinner buckets like that. Well, in the wintertime. You know, those of us that carried coffee or if you had milk or any other liquid that would freeze solid in there. Well, there was a few places around the plant where they had steam traps would discharge, you know, into a barrel. And of course, that water was boiling hot all the time. Well, when that noon whistle had blow, well, first of all, you had to get in line to punch the time clock. You had to punch in the seven in the morning. You had to punch these time clocks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seven in the morning, 12 noon, one and then six.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Six at night, ten hour day. Well, 12:00 noon. If you happen to get quite a ways back in line for this time clock and your coffee was frozen solid, well, then by the time he got over to this barrel of hot water, there'd be a line up there. And then you had to wait your turn to get up there and hold this tree, this container of coffee down in this hot water to where you could throw it and heat it, you know, to where you like to have it. Then you didn't have much time left. Then you see, to eat your lunch. And of course, there was no way to throw your sandwiches, you know, and there was a few spots around where, of course, there they could set their lunch box so that it wouldn't wouldn't freeze. You know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The men were working in below freezing temperatures.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Sure, man. You know. You know, if these panty waists nowadays, you can't even describe what working conditions were. You had to be tough and take it or die. That was there was no alternative at all. It was. It was just either. Well, where plenty of clothes and everybody wore lots of wool, wool underwear, wool socks, wool mittens, wool shirts, world pants and cover your ears up the best you could. And of course, where you handled lumber. Why, you'd have wool mittens inside of leather mittens, you see. And even then your fingers had just freeze because that lumber was cold as could be, just like handling ice. But. They had had two power plants here. The power plant that's in operation there still. And then they had one that that run the planing mill. Well, there was a few men that would make a dash for in the boiler room where it was always pretty warm and they would sit in there and eat their lunch. But these firemen, they'd bellyache like everything about that, because they got so many men in their, you know, that they'd interfere with their work, you know, and, but anyway, there'd be quite a few of them that would pile up in the boiler rooms.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But there was an awful lot of the, the men that didn't have anywhere else to go. If they carried the lunch, they just sit down on a cold stack of lumber and ate a frozen sandwich. And, and. If they had some, some liquid coffee, tea or milk or whatever it was, why they tried to get that thawed out and if they'd otherwise, they just eat a no on a cold sandwich.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the meal more or less quiet during that lunch hour?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah. Yeah, they shut down. Everything was shut down. You see, they had they had this big Corliss engine that drove the sawmill 1200 horsepower, and then they had an 800 horsepower engine that drove the planing mill. And of course, when they'd shut that engine down, well, then everything there's nothing moved then, because nowadays they have individual power on each unit, you know, they can start and stop any unit, but then you either run everything or you didn't. That was it. Of course they had, as I say, no, they they would run everything. You could still stop units and start units through a clutch system, but everything, you know, it was all belt driven and you could throw in a clutch and then start a unit, or you could throw the clutch out and and stop a unit. But when they did stop for the noon hour or for the end of the shift, well then of course everything was stopped. You didn't move anything because you couldn't move anything unless that engine was running, see?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was quiet enough. Then the men could sit down and talk to me.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Sure. Of course. Now, the only place where you couldn't talk to each other during the workday would be inside of the sawmill itself. And inside of the planer. Of course, there they talked to each other, too, but they would have to get to lips right up to a man's ear, you know, in order to talk to them. But. There'd be. Oh, you take no, they but one third of the loading dock where they loaded their cars would be in this planing mill area, and there it was too noisy. But the other two thirds of the loading dock was out where you could talk, just like in normally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened when the temperature got down, as it does sometimes to below zero in in Potlatch. Would it, would it do men be able to change at all or did you just have to to stand it? I mean did they.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Have to stand it? You know, there's I don't exaggerate at all when I say that. I've seen it below 40 degrees below zero. That's cold. And. There was oh, they'd show some leniency, but I have seen it too, in cases where. In extreme cold, if a man didn't report for work, why, he just lost his job. Because, you know, the operation had to keep on going. And if you couldn't take it. Well, then they didn't need you there anymore. See? So more or less, people had to be tough and you had to dress for it. And. You take ten hours out there in that cold weather. I think the planning mill at that time was probably the most severe place to be. They had. Well, they had 22 machines in the planer, so that was a pretty long, you know, building. And it had a sheet metal roof on it, but it was opened on the sides. And of course it had a big overhanging roof, you know, so that everybody stayed pretty much in the dry. But that wind could go right through it, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then, of course, each machine had these. Would call a blow pipes, but they were suction pipes, you know, that sucked all these shavings away from the machine, you know, and up to the system, of course, that in the South would create an awful lot of movement of air. Well, these machine, each one of these painters. There was two men. One man would feed the machine and the other one would take away from it to see. And the men that took away from the machine were usually a lumber greater. And these boards come out of the machine. He would have to grade that. That lumbers had come out, you know, put it on grade so that it had to be accurate. And he would have one narrow gauge railroad car on this side of his bench that is had come out onto a bench and he's all the lumber was on grade on this order, you see, would go on to this one over here and and all the outs.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That is everything that was other than this grade would go on to this unit over here. So he just stood there. He didn't have any. The only way he could even move his feet to help the circulation in his feet would be to stand there and tap dance or something, you know, and he just stood in one place and handed these boards over this way, in this way. You know, when you do that for ten, ten hours a day in freezing weather and I'll tell you, you're taking off a beating.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wouldn't that be true for a lot of the guys in a in the mill, as compared to the guys working in the woods? I would think that in the early days in the woods, they'd be naturally moving a lot more.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. They'd be moving, they'd be walking and so on. And, you know, lots of movement. But and of course, now you're taking the sawmill itself. It was it was always fairly warm in the sawmill because it was entirely enclosed. And then, of course, everything in the sawmill was operated by steam. So there was a lot of radiation there, you see… and… now in this particular mill… they had… awful high ceilings. For one thing, there was 20ft from the from the floor, the bottom floor up to the saw floor. So that pretty high ceiling in the lower level. So you will end the upper level. Well then that was open then up to the roof area and of course that roofless sloping. So there was no ceiling in there you know. But they had, they had double row of ventilators, windows, these windows that would open up the full length of the building in order to give them ventilation in there and relieve some of that heat. So it was usually pretty warm in the sawmill, but it was the men in the planning mill and the loading area, the loading docks and the lumber yard that was really subjected to this cold in the in the wintertime, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds like the people in plane mill had it both ways, because the noise being so high, as well as the being so cold.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, there are a lot of men that worked in the air all their lives that lost their hearing, you know, and kind of that high pitched noise. And of course, there was a lot of men that it didn't affect them that bad. It there was a difference in, in the human being, you know, just what these different organs can take… but… there were some of them that would just go stone deaf, you know, from the noise. Well, now days. It's it's state and federal law is that any work area that has any noise at all, you know that. Well then they've got to where your ear plugs or ear protection, you know, they were these muffs. You know these.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there wasn't back then.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh they didn't. Of course they never they never heard television, never even thought about any safety practices. You know, you take as far as you take evenly really high. Risk areas of the industrial plant. They never even thought about building any guards or anything else. You know, it was it was up to you to watch where you were stepping and where you put your hands in your head and so on. And in fact, the matter is, you know, they didn't seem like they were responsible. You were responsible to yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were the high danger spots?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Anywhere where there was machinery running. They had the sawmill, planing mill and a box factory. Of course, that box factory, it wasn't built until about 1912, but in the box factory they had. Oh, just ever so many cut off saws and rip saws and all that stuff, you know. And men, every once in a while you'd hear about some guy losing these fingers, you know, and but and of course, everything was built, driven. And of course, there was quite a hazard there, you know, getting wound up in a belt. It's kind of hard to get someone to realize the. All the proportions that were used in putting up a plant like they had here. For instance. Now, I say this big Corliss engine that drove the mill. It had a flywheel on it that was had five feet across the face of the flywheel, and it was 24ft in diameter, all heavy cast iron. Of course, that was all put together in in sections, you know, bolted together. But anyway, this huge flywheel and this belt that was on this, on this big wheel that went from there to into the sawmill, it took 3000 cow hides to make that one belt.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So you can imagine then get some idea of the proportions you see that was used. Of course, when I when you see a cowhide that was just the backs, they don't take the flanks or nothing because they just use that back of the cow hide. But anyway, it was three hides thick. The belt was about so thick, you see, and and five feet wide.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long about was it, you know well.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Approximately 150ft, I'd say. I can't give you the exact length of it. You'd have to take half the diameter, that flywheel, and then from, from the center, that flywheel to the center line of the shaft in the sawmill was about… i'd say… 50 or 60ft. And then, of course, the the driven, driven pulley in the sawmill. It was about. Approximately six feet in diameter or something like that. So of course, you see that would that would increase your speed ratio, you see, from this big flywheel to the smaller one. Well then the. The main drive shaft in the sawmill. That come off in this big engine there, that's chapter seven inches in diameter. And then of course. From that shaft, well, then there was several counter shafts, you know, there would be big belts that would drive over onto a counter shaft. And then that shaft probably would drive on to another counter shaft. So it was quite an intricate layout of, of transmission of power, you know, in order to power that whole mill off in this one power unit, you know, often this one engine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, it, it was laid out in such a way that, it was all in the open and men could get tangled up in it pretty easy if they didn't watch this.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Well… i can recall when. In the structure, the wooden structure of the mill itself. Of course, they had these wooden columns, you know, they were, 16in square, these columns, you know, and then, of course, they had these big timbers that went between the columns they called bridge trees. It's what they were called. Well, anyway, they were about 24ft apart. And of course, there was a great big bearing on each one of those for this shaft. And you see. Well, the man now see, that was the Euler. By, oh, for a long time, while he'd carry ladder with him and he'd go up here and all this bearing, and then he'd move over and he'd another bearing. So what did they do then? They just after a while, they just laid a plank, a single plank, maybe four inches thick. But anyway, the single plank from this bridge tree over to that bridge tree, 24ft span there, you see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then from that one to the next one. So he would just walk along that plank, and he held this bearing, and then he'd walk around and all the next one and so on. Well, of course with these big pulleys were this plank had to run through past the face of that pulley. Well, then from this plank, he would just walk on that bridge tree over to the bearing, which would probably be some places. Maybe he'd have to go step over there 4 or 5ft. Maybe another place would be 10 or 12ft over. But when he was walking over there, here was this pulley running right alongside of him here, all in the open sea. So if he would lose his balance, he could fall right into that pulley. So you see, a man had to be watch what he was doing all the time. Of course, they wouldn't even think about letting them plant even start up nowadays in conditions like that. You know, if they everything wasn't guarded before they even started, they wouldn't even let them start. But that's they didn't know any better. And everybody was happy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they. The men were men. Didn't think twice about it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh no. No. In fact, the matter is, you know, you take this job that was sort of a premium job. You know, it always paid. Probably $0.05 an hour above common and and of course, you wasn't tied down to one spot, you know, and you had more freedom and and. You know, follow it. Be kind of tickled, you know, to get a job. Well, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people think of it as a dangerous job as compared to the others in the mill?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh… you know, the average person, in fact, I might say, everyone, when you first go into the sawmill, it'd scare you to death. The noise and all the movement going on in there, you know. But what are you going to do? Golly, you know, you're you're looking for a job. You ask for a job, and you're willing to take anything to get a job because you've got to support yourself and your family if you've got a family. So what do you do? You just grit your teeth. Well, nowadays they they won't even think about putting a person on a production job until he's had some job training. That's one of the the. Things that's required nowadays is you've got to have give every man job training. But those days they they didn't. They would if you'd never been inside of a sawmill in your life, you didn't have any idea what it looked like in their. And you went over and you asked the foreman for a job. And it so happened that he needed a fellow safe to pick engines. Now, when I say pick edges, that's behind the edger. These down here, they had double edges.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That is one machine took care of two head rigs and it was two men feeding it. Well then just beyond this edgier right in this role case for these roles are just traveling like mad. You know, they had a an area that was opened up between two roles, and it had a platform there for a man to stand on. These boards come out, this edger, this man had to flip this edge off, probably two sides of that board down onto this transfer that carried the edges of way. See. And then of course, he had to go here. And they flipped these boards off in that side. See because he had ten. Well that was he had a space in there about six feet. He'd have to go this way and go that way. Well now you're a new man. Had never been in the sawmill in life. And he'd say, yeah, I need an engine picker. You can go to work up there and say, well, all right. Never. He didn't even know what an edge and picker was. So he'd go up there and he'd have some man in there, say, temporarily, until they could find a new man doing this job. And he'd probably let you stand there for five minutes and watch that fella.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: What are you doing? And then he'd. He'd probably give the edge of the high sign to hold up for a minute. Long enough for that fella to jump out, and you jump in, and then you started doing it. That's the training you got. Well, now these boards come out of there. If you didn't keep a keen eye, maybe one of them boards had come along and they hit you just about just above the knees there you see. And boy, they could hit your hard. And in fact, the matter is there has been fellows that was hit hard enough to boost them, clear out of that pit and down onto the roll case. But it's up to you to keep a keen eye. Well. These these edges, they had four movable saws on this end of the shaft, and they had four movable saws on this end of the shaft. Well, there was a space in between here of about. About three feet where there wasn't any size. Well, this lumber coming, if that didn't board, didn't have to be edged. They just shoved it down through the middle there because it didn't have to be edged, you see. Well, here you're standing in that pit over there with boards coming at you two sides. But then this joker on this one, the edge, or he sends one down through the middle.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So where are you going to go? The only thing you can do is jump out of there, try to get out of the way. And don't you have to have to do that real quick? It was tricky work and you know, it was. It wasn't it wasn't at all call for that. Anything like that should ever happen. But there was individuals that got a big kick out of trying to see somebody scramble, you know, and they even got a kick out of it. They see somebody got hurt. You know, there was this just kind of a bloodthirsty. Bunch of people. There was, of course, I'd say the majority of them were all real kind people, but there was a few. You'd always find some individual, some joker in the in the crowd, you know, that's that's got to do something like that. You know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think guys like that would do something like that because they were just frustrated and their work, or you think they were just mean?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course, you know, if they were psychoanalyst, you could probably find a lot of answers as to why they'd done those things, you know? But. Why would a guy just walk up behind you with a cold glass of water and pour it down the back of your neck? They might do that today. You know, if he was down here, say, swimming… hole and… probably sitting there on a bench watching the kids swim and maybe some joker come up behind you with a cold bottle of pop and poured down the back of your neck. Why would they do that? You know, it's kind of hard to answer why people do some of these things. And yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it's one thing to play practical joke on something. It's another thing to see somebody get hurt and get them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I know, but then it's there's some people do that doesn't realize what a practical joke will do and how far it might carry. You see. You know, they used to cut a lot of yellow pine lumber here. And this yellow pine, there was a lot of knots would fall out, you know, particularly a one inch board and big notch in a way. Maybe they're not would fall out. Well, you'd find it always a lot of knots on the floor, you know, just needs areas wherever, wherever the lumber was transferred to be, not fall out. But you taken an area where it's noisy and a man is concentrated on concentrating on his own work. And all of a sudden, here comes a knock flying through the air, and it takes you in the back of the head or in your back, or maybe in front. And you look around and see where who threw that not. And of course, everybody's got a long face and doing his job. See, because some joker picked up a knot and we looked down through there and then of course, turned right around to his work.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And you can't see where he'd come from, see? But why does people do that? See what they used to do a lot of that throwing knots and, you know, you could get hurt. And you take a, a knot is it isn't round and smooth like a ball would be. A knot is going to sharp edges on it and not can cut. You know, it can cut like a knife and oh there's been lots of them, you know blooded run. We're not hit them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that… a matter of trying to see how somebody could take it? Or do you think they go after somebody who they thought was soft and try to get rid of them?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That kind of. No, I think I think they they just try to get a reaction out of a person. And of course, the more you react to a practical joke, the more you'd be subject to the practical jokes, because they say, we'll get a raise out of that guy. You watch now. We'll see. Now see how he acts. He. And then they'll pull some joke on him, see? And and of course, if, if you can just laugh it off and don't over react to it, but then they leave you alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a difference between the kind of practical jokes that hurt you with a knot thrown on your back and other kinds? It just didn't physical at all?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, there was in fact, a matter of urgency. You take practical jokes. That was very common. They had all seemed like they was inventing new ones every day, but most of them were harmless, you know, it was there something to irritate somebody, you know, and that is there at least try to irritate somebody. And of course, some people are more sensitive than others, you know, and and some of them really get riled up over it, you know. And then of course, they had whoop and holler and laugh and poke fun at him after they get him mad, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what kinds of I used to job, what kind of ways they job people in?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, it's kind of hard to recall just all of them that they had during many of them, you know that. Oh, I know, I know one thing that. Now it seems kind of funny. And at the time it could have probably hurt somebody. But this box factory that they had here, they built that in 1912. And for the first couple of years or so there I, they nailed all their boxes by hand. Of course, they had power saws to cut all these pieces, you know, cleats and so on. You know, and ripped them to thin boards and all that stuff. But then the nailing part was all done by hand. They had several big long rows, benches, you know, there were these men would I say men, kids and men. They had their nailing up boxes and. They got, oh, I can remember they got $0.35 a hundred boxes for nailing boxes. You nailed up a hundred boxes, you got $0.35. And of course, some of these guys were strong and and, you know, they developed a good, good arm for handling. They had done the nailing with the with these.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Hatchets. You know, they didn't use a hammer. And so of course you can. You swing them all day long. You developed quite an arm. But anyway with the all these saws running and all this hammering and banging and going on, you know, there was an awful lot of noise. And I can recall several times where be some practical joker would sneak up behind a guy, you know, and you stand there nailing, working like, everything, you know. And this guy would take one of these nails and he'd just go down through the the soul of his shoe, you know, that sticking out from his shoe. And he'd nearly shoot out of the floor. And, you know, he turned to get some more cleats or or boards, pieces for the box. And, boy, he'd fall flat on his face because he couldn't move his foot. See? But of course, you know, you could break a man's leg, I suppose, to that way if he turned right. But then. Then they never did that I ever heard of. But stuff like that, that's that's a real practical joke, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And then possibly of some really harmless thing, but irritating, like, everything. Maybe guy lays gloves down and when he'd come back, while he would pick up his gloves and stick his hand in there, maybe some guy put a bunch of Greece or something down inside his glove, and he'd stick his hand into that, you know? But oh, there was one of different things that was done, you know, but it seemed like most, most everybody had a pretty good sense of humor. And, you know, if. If you would play a joke on me, well, then I'd probably lay awake nights for a month trying to figure out some way where I could get back and play a joke on you, you see?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If I knew you'd done it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, yeah, I even had the. Good suspicious suspicion,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I look on the guy's face.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know… i can recall one incident on there in the planar. They. The whole plant was was covered with these narrow gauge railroads. They moved all their lumber on these little railroad cars, you see, and. So in the planer, there was I said there was 22 machines in there, and each machine had a switch and a rail coming in to the machine, you see. And then of course, there was, there was two sets of rails going out from each machine out onto the loading dock area. But anyway, all this lumber was transported by horsepower. They had one horse and a driver, and and he would hook a chain onto this. Unit of lumber. And he would they would probably be several cars here. And he'd go back and say 5 or 6 cars back and hook on to that. And as he started up, they'd moved this whole bunch down this track, see, but in this planer area, they had electric locomotives that brought all the lumber, that took all the green lumber out to the lumber area, the lumber yard, and then they and then they brought all the dry lumber from the lumber yard back into the planer.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And they had I think they had seven of those electric locomotives. And but then, of course, they would they would leave this whole string of, of loads in on this main, main line there. And then this man of the horse, he would pull these loads into the different machines, wherever they're supposed to go into the planer. Well, there was a fellow by name of Kearney that drove this horse back there, and he was a nicest fella in the world. Everybody liked him. He was such a pleasant sort of a guy, you know, and saw it come to a time there when, by golly, men would pick up their lunch box at noon. And by Gus, you know, they knew that their wife had made a nice pie and they, they really expected the piece of pie in their lunch, and it wasn't there. And when they'd come home, they'd ask their wife, how come you didn't give me some that pie? Well, she says, I did. I put some in there. Well, it wasn't there. Well, maybe the next guy he wife had made a nice cake or or or something extra, you know, to put in the end. Of course. Lunchtime. Come. It wasn't there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you know, everybody just would set their lunch box down here or there on a on this building was all steel structure, you see, is still the girders and columns and everything. And then of course, it's sheet iron covered. And of course, wherever there'd be a steel beam or something there, they set their lunch box up there and wouldn't pay attention to it until noon. So everybody got to suspicion somebody else around there, you know, who was stealing their lunch, but they never could catch him and buddy. So there was one guy that said he was going to find out. So he had his wife to make a nice pie. And then he went to the drugstore and he got some quilting. And so after she cut this nice wedge of pie where he put some croton oil in it and had it right on top of his lunch, you know, and no one come by here, that pie was gone.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So then he figured, well, we soon find out who had that pie. Well, it just turned out that poor old carny that guy had driving the horse. The nicest guy in the whole plant was the guy that's stealing the lunch. And, you know, he had died running to the John, you know? You know, he got such a diarrhea, you know, from that croton, all that just he was over done it. But that's the way the guy found that. Well, that was a practical joke that that, of course, still in these lunches, you know, and that went on for it was quite a period of time, you know, and, and there was a lot of fellows that complained to that. They could see that somebody had been in their lunch box, but there was nothing taken. See. But he'd he'd go alone. He'd look at each one and he'd, he'd go back to the one that had the whatever he wanted to see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened after that. Did did he leave or did he stay?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, I remember, I think he stayed, but then it cured him of of this stealing and of course, all the knowledge of who it was. It didn't really get to widespread. They didn't they didn't. They kind of felt sorry for him afterwards, you know… but.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever see fights in.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I, I actually only saw about two, I guess maybe all the years I was there, but then there was more there. But then I didn't really get to see them. But I did get to see a couple of them. And of course they all those that I saw and I guess the others too, that they really. They really didn't last too long or didn't amounted to much, you know, they'd maybe one guy had get knocked down and then he'd quit and that'd be the end of it. You know, it wasn't like the loggers, you know, the. And I don't think the loggers out in this area ever got anywhere near as tough as they were back in the Midwest. Seemed like the loggers all got along pretty good on this part of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know why that would be the difference.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, I think so. It was. You might you might say. It was the nationality of the people that that worked in the woods. Now, you take back in the Midwest. They had Canadian Frenchmen and they had a few Irish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Finns you mentioned.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And the the Finns and the Swedes. Now, the poles never worked in the woods. Poles all worked, always used to work around the sawmill unit. And and their work usually consisted of piling slab wood or or or some job or it didn't take a brain to do anything, you know. And I never seen her tell her, one of them ever learning to grade lumber or anything like that, but. The people from the Scandinavian peninsula, there, they were the natural lumbermen, loggers, lumbermen. Because that was. Well, that that was an industry that had carried on for hundreds and hundreds of years over there before it ever started over here. You take there's one sawmill over there in Sweden that has been in continuous operation for over 700 years… and… in in a sense, I guess it's about the biggest sawmill operation in the world.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You could wonder, you know, why? How a such a small country could produce that much timber. But of course they don't. They produce a lot of timber, because you might say every time they cut down a tree, they had to plant a tree. But. You see, they shipped their Tim, they shipped their timber in across the water from Finland. And from Latvia and Estonia. And some of those countries have belonged to Russia. Now, you know, there was lots of timber and that was all shipped over to Sweden. So you see these Swedes, they come by this logging and lumbering, more or less natural because they were brought up with it. Now, the Norwegians were different. They had they had some… but… from what I find out by. They wasn't very well supplied with timber in Norway because that was such a mountainous country, you know, and crags and rocks, and that was more or less of a fishing industry in Norway, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how does this go with how they got along in the woods and didn't fight out here as compared to the Midwest?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well… you take where you have like back there. You had, as I said, there's French Canadians and Finns and Swedes and Irish. When you get you get a conglomerate like that, you're bound to have a lot of friction and a lot of trouble. The fact of the matter is, maybe that can start and probably usually start by one guy poking fun at this other guy because he didn't speak good English or. And there was always, like I said here the other day, that it seems like the. The Irish were arrogant, they were terribly arrogant and their behavior. And that didn't set good with some of these others, you know, you see. Back in the, in the area there. You'd see an Irishman while you seen a man that his aspiration was to be a foreman and. How he got to be a foreman was because he could whip every man in the crew. That was the only qualification he needed. He didn't need to know anything about logging or lumbering or anything else. The only way to be a foreman was to be able to whip every man the crew. Well, maybe he did whip a guy.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But then when Saturday night come and they got down to saloon. He took a beating because maybe one guy wouldn't take him, but maybe 4 or 5 of them would see and they'd tromp him right into the ground. But that was, you know, one reason. And there was a lot of different reasons, you know, that could bring it about. But anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But out here, there was more similarity in nationality among the lumberjacks. Then there was back there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More more Swedes and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, there was, there was. Of course, there was a lot of men working in the in the woods here too, that wasn't of the Scandinavian descent. But then there was an awful lot of them. It was, you know, you'd take like old Axel Anderson and some of those guys, you know. Well, they had the choice of hiring and firing. Well, you know, as well as I know that. If a Swede come up to Axel Anderson probably couldn't speak a word of English. See, but he's looking for a job, actually give him a job. So consequently, this camp would be pretty well made up of a bunch of Swedes, you know? And of course, there'd be others. Do you know, that was, you might say, American born, that of different nationality, but they'd be working there, too. But they were all men that were pretty easy to get along with. It wasn't high tempered and hot blooded like he'd found back in the Midwest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where most of the camp foreman Scandinavian out here.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I'd say most of them. No, I wouldn't say that. But then there was a lot of them that were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back there in the Midwest. They were mostly Irish.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Or mostly. Yeah. And, as I say, the reason for that was because they could they were fighting men. Irishmen had been known for fighting men for hundreds of years back, because that's where the Swedes, all they knew was to cut logs and make lumber and farm the Irish. All they knew was to fight and get drunk. That's that's a I know that's going on tape, but nevertheless, that's a, that's a just a natural heritage. Why? And it prevails right to this day, far as that goes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See when I, I'm very interested in, in, in this difference because I've read about the fighting went on in the Midwest and I've talked to lots of loggers out here and I haven't heard much about fights and the lumber camps, anything like what I've heard about back there. So to me, I believe there is a strong difference between here and there. And I really wondered about why.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, and of course, the. In order to get the lumber industry moving in this part of the world, you had to bring people from back there, out here as a nucleus, you know, to, to build this thing up. But the people that come out here wasn't that type. Now, I believe I told you the other day that these people migrated out here when they heard that they're building this plant or building some plant in Sandpoint or somewhere else. These French-Canadians, they didn't come out here. If they run out of a job, say, in Wisconsin, where they go, they went back to Quebec. And and then most of the Irish, there was a few that come out, but they, they were in such a minority that they, they didn't cause trouble. But most of them, they wanted to stay back there in, you might say, the more populated part of the country. And there was reasons for, for, for migration, you know.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The majority of them had come out in this part of the country. They come out here because. There was more money, they got paid more, and living conditions were better. And then others come out here because they wanted eventually to get some land. They wanted a home. They were something that come out here, you know, that their job at mill. Was their livelihood. But they bought a stump branch because they wanted a piece of ground. And just any number of reasons why people come out, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was would you would you agree that there was some fighting in the, in the lumber camps here, but just not very much.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, there was some there bound to be. But of course, in this potlatch area, I think one reason that held the fighting down a lot was that that liquor wasn't quite as available as it was back in the Midwest, and but then I think too, that to here. There was a good many cases, I believe, that were two men would have a difference that would bring them to the point of fighting. If it was in the camp area itself. I think there was a good many times where they were both given their time and packed down the road. You just get out of here. We don't want any fighting men around here at all. And, I think that held it down a lot too, because word, it word will get out. You know that boy, if you go to work for that guy in that camp, you'd better not start any fights. Because if you do, you're done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the presence in the camps of a lot of men who are local, local people who were, let's say, stomp, stomp farmers and were working there for part of the year or working there to clear up their place, that that had a settling influence on the camps, too.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. I think that, men are not going to jeopardize their job by letting their tempers get the best of them, providing that is, if their loss of their job is going to mean the loss of their home and having to move to another locality or move their family to another locality. It's a stabilizing factor, you know, that,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about some of these different nationalities that were in Potlatch? I understand that especially for common labor, that large numbers of Japanese and large numbers of Greeks were were brought in here in the early years.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. Well, I don't know if they were brought in, but then they come in because of the same factor that brought other people in here. And of course, there might have been a lot of them that were contracted, you know, from the coast or someplace, and they contracted them to come over here. But as I was saying here at the beginning when we started talking, you know, about the the lay of the land when they built this plant or this little more or less of an island down there that is a created an island by diverting the river around. You see, because the original river bed run right around the foothill here, you see well, on that island there they built the sawmill. And then at this southern end of it, they built this great big barn for their horses. And then right next to the barn they had away and corral. That's something else to do that reverts back from the old times to was a wagon corral.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You probably heard of it, but it was a board fence that was about eight feet high, and the boards were perpendicular, 12 inch boards, and they were laid tight together. So it was a solid fence. The only way you could see in there would be find a knothole to see inside. And of course they had this big gate for opening it up, and they'd drive their wagons in and all their wagons were put in there at night, you see. And that was the wagon. Well, that was right next to the the horse barn. And right next to that was the baseball park. They had really good baseball park here, and it had a eight foot board fence all around it to the entire park. You couldn't you had to find a knothole to see in there, to let you paid you way in. But there's a little strip of this island here, back of this barn. And alongside this baseball park where they had the some buildings. That was the original buildings that they put up when they first started construction, that is, they had to store the. Well, it was a commissary, you might say, and bunkhouses and so on for these construction crews lived down down there.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, after they got their houses built here in town. What, what finally was the town? Well, then they they still had these buildings down there. And that's where they had one big building there. That was a Jap camp. All the Japs lived there. Then they had some of these other buildings that had been bunkhouses and so on. Were all the Italians lived. And then there was just a few of them there where all the Greeks lived. There wasn't any of the Greeks or Italians or the Japs that lived in the town itself. They all lived down there until… about,… oh, I guess about 1918. Then they built this big boarding house here. Well, on the hillside just above this Standard Oil tank station down here. For the Japs. And then they had one street there, that the entire street was all Italians and Greeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So even after 1918, when they built those, it was still segregated, still separate from the rest of the of the community.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. But then that was that was there, you might say, their own choice. Now, of course, the the Japs wanted to live by themselves because of their moldy living. As I say, they had this big boarding house. And of course, there was there was a few Jap families that had little houses, but they was still on that lower street. There were the Greeks, and the Italians were. But these Japs, most of them were single. And of course, most of the Japs worked in the box factory. But they had a cook, and this cook would heat up a great big tub of water. This tub was a big wooden tub. And it. Oh, I guess it stood 3.5ft high for such a matter. And it was about. Between 8 and 10ft in diameter, I guess, and it was full of water. And then, of course, they had a fire box underneath of that thing. It had a steel bottom in.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And this cook, he would build up a fire and stoke that up and get this huge tub of water heated up. Well then as soon as their shift was over with, these Japs come home. And that's one thing about a Jap terror where he lives, or if he lives in the jungle or in the sawmill, he takes a bath every day. But I don't think they ever cared too much about changing the water. As long as they take a bath. And, And they all pile into that tub. As many as could get in there one time, and they'd jabber and talk and go on and lay everything and and then, of course, as soon as they wash off a little bit and that tub, well, they jump out and then maybe the rest of them would jump in. But then way they didn't have that many people there. But what maybe, maybe two jumps would be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many, how many could get in that tub? And once about.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, probably, 15 or 20. Get in there. One time they'd stand up in there, you know, and slosh around. And I don't know if they ever changed the water. They probably did. But, but anyway, it's just like a raccoon washing these food. And, you know, he might have a clean carrot as the cleanest can be, but he'll slosh it around in a mud puddle to wash it before you lead it. And so that's the way those folks were. But then they. The Italians and the Greeks, of course, they they all spoke their own language, and they wanted to be together. And the Japs, they talked to Japanese and a few of them could talk just a little bit of the American language enough to get by, and they would talk for the rest of them. And, and the same with the Italians… it'd be maybe… 30 or 40 Italians and maybe 2 or 3 of them were married, but one of them done the business for all of them, you see. And.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, each of each of these groups had its its leader who took care of the business.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. That is, you know, if you take the Italians especially, there are people over in the old country were so poor, you know, that a dollar would be like a million to anybody else. Well, these fellas working here, every payday, they would send a little bit of money back to the old country, see? Well. It seemed like they didn't know whether it might be that they didn't have enough education, you know, to write their name or to make out of money order. So they would designate one person to he would take all this money to the post office, and he would send so much to this one and so much to that one, and so much of that one… and… it it's kind of strange when you look into the background of people. We're living in a so-called modern times, but, we're not too far removed from the primitive as far as that's concerned.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I can there was there was one family. Of course, everybody thought he was an Italian, but he was Austrian. He'd come Austria and he was a really a nice fella. He was. He run one of these big steam engines down here at the plant for a good many years… and they had a fellow that boarded with them for good many years. This, this engineer, he had a he had a wife and he had one boy. But anyway, there was a fellow that boarded with him for years and years and years, and he was an awful nice sort of fella. But,… seemed like when this, this guy was boarding with this other family, when he left the old country, he had a sweetheart back there, and she was just a little slim slip of a girl, you know, real pretty. And so he promised her that he'd come to America. And then as soon as he could, why, he'd send for her.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And so she agreed. So he come over here and. And so he tried time and again, you know, to to get her to come over. But she wouldn't come by herself. She was scared to go by herself. So consequently, while he was up pretty well in middle age, when he finally decided that he was going to honor his commitment, you know, that he he promised her that he would marry her, and she was still waiting for him back there… so he finally… went back there and was going to marry her and then bring it, bring her back here. When he got back there, he found that his sweetheart was a huge, fat woman. Barefooted. She had never had a pair of shoes in her life. And this whole village where they lived. There was one person in the whole village that could read or write. So whenever they would receive a letter, they would have to take this letter over to this person and have them read it for them. And if they was going to be a reply, well, then they would tell that person what they'd like to write. That person would write the letter and mail it to them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that's the way they lived over in that country, you see. Well, this guy said that, gosh, when he came over there, of course, he had on nice clothes. And of course, the people in that part of the world, even right today. They think that all Americans are millionaires. You know, there's. When you come over to America, all you have to do if you need money is to go out here on the bush and pick it off the bush. You see, that's what that's what they believe over there. So they all thought he was a millionaire. And he said that there was just any number of real young, real good looking girls and women that that wanted him to marry them, you know, and bring them over here. But of course, he had committed himself to this other woman. So. He said that, it was it was in the summertime when he went back there. And they were they would dry their fruit. They had lots of apricots and that sort of fruit, you know, and they would dry them. But he asked them why they didn't catch them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They looked at him. They didn't even know what he was talking about. So he tried to explain to him about getting these glass jars and preserving their fruit that way, more natural flavor to it when they'd eat it. And they never heard of it. They never even seen it, you know. Of course they I guess they'd seen some canned fruit in tin cans, you know, that was shipped in there. So he said that he was going to. Go down at the store and get some some jars and going to show them how to can some of this fruit. And the story of this little store there, they didn't know what he did. They said that they could get him some bottles. He said no, he didn't want bottles. He wanted fruit carries. No, they didn't have any. So he had to go to another larger town. He said, this is about, I don't know, 50 or 60 miles away to another town is bigger. And he he did get some food and brought it back and showed him how to put up some of this fruit.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But anyway. In the meantime, he was getting ready to marry this woman. And of course he he had to get her a pair of shoes and they put her killed her, you know, trying to wear shoes she never wore. She was in her life. And. Then, of course, he he married her and and he brought her over here and he built a house over here. Just over here on the way. But that woman could neither read nor write. Nobody in the whole village could read or write, except that one person. And I think that person was a priest. But. That's one reason why, when they come here to work, they wanted to stay pretty much together. It kind of looked like the people of the town didn't want them living intermingled with the rest of the town, but I don't believe that was the case at all. I think that it was a case that they lived that way, their own choice, because they could jabber and talk in their own language, and they were happy that way.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And,… i think the Japs, I think they, they had better education, but it was,… it was in the Japanese education. And of course, they're their mode of writing and so on. Their, their script is so much different from ours, you know, that, that they, they couldn't most of them couldn't read or write our language, but still they had they'd been educated in their own country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the Japanese, kept a lot of their old country customs here?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I think they kept them all. I think they kept them all. There. Of course, they didn't have any. They didn't have any temples or anything like that. But still, I think each Japanese family I think, believe has a shrine right in their own home, you know, and, you know, they have idols and images and one thing, whatever they worshiped the and so I think they they carried on all their own traditions wherever they go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know that the Chinese had a very hard time of it in the early days, like in a mining camps. And around there, you know, they did all the they mine the tailings and they were the, the labor, the heavy labor. And I know that that the old miners say and I and I've read and people feel, you know, that the Chinese were just looked down upon like almost animals.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm wondering if it was that way with the Japanese, too.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they were they were. I'm not going to deny that. And of course, you take it's a funny thing you take United States is is made up and it's populated with just a conglomerate of of every. Race and creed in the world, you know, makes up the population of the United States. But nevertheless, it seems like. Oh, it it always was very common for you'd hear some somebody would make a… derogatory remark about that dumb Swede and that damn sweet and that stupid Swede and all that sort of thing. Well, then, of course, is that whenever they talk about the Italians, it was always that dago, you know, they had you never heard for years and years. You never heard tell of anybody, say, an Italian. It was always a dago, you see, or a walk, you know, they'd refer to them as a and,… of course, there were some, some people that probably didn't have $0.02 to their name.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That if, if an Italian family was going to move in next door, they'd feel he feel really put out about it because they didn't want that dago living next door to him. See? Well, that so-called dago that probably moved next door, probably financially and every other way, and much better off in this person was over here. But that's the way people are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there was mixing among the groups and and between them and the, let's say the Italians and Japanese and the population, the general population of Potlatch was that really kept to a minimum. I mean, wasn't minimal or.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I tell you, I don't I think I don't believe there was there was I don't believe there's any friction between the races at all because. Oh, it seemed like each one respected the other one, you know, and each one more or less stayed in their, their own compound. And, you know, they didn't mingle too much, although on the job, they probably work right side by side on the job and just get along just fine… you know this… but, well, it always seemed to me at least that. If there was any prejudice, it seemed to me like it was probably more on the other side because these minority groups, they didn't want to mingle with the others. That's what it seemed to me like, you know, the reason for it might have been that they might have felt that, well, that they were out of place, they wasn't wanted or something like that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, you take off. They had a public. Doing of some kind. Maybe they'd say, for instance, they had a in the early days they used to have these ice cream socials and basket socials and one thing or another, well, those people could have come and they would have accepted their money like anybody else's, but they didn't come because they just felt that they was out of place. You know, it was their own choice that they didn't come. I know for for fact, of course we don't. We don't belong to the Catholic Church. But then I know that they've had a Catholic church here, you might say, ever since the town started. And you take all of these. Italians, then the Austrians that were here from bordering on to Italy, they were all Catholics, you know, and, you know, that was the Catholic stronghold for 2000 years.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But you never see one of them go to the Catholic Church. The reason they didn't go was because these other snooty women that went to church there, they didn't want to mingle with them, but that is at least they thought that was the case, you see. So they didn't go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where most of them single men were there, many women, many wives.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There was. There was some. Yeah, there was a few. But then what would I say? Most of them were single men. I think I told you the other day about the. Approximate number of people in this town. They'd kept borders, you know. Well, you take about three fourths of the houses in town, would keep anywhere from two to 5 or 6 borders besides hotel being full and a big hotel annex and and a big boarding house and a big boarding house annex full of rooms, all single men. And beside all these people that was keeping boarders. So you can see there was a big percentage of them with single men, or at least men that weren't living with their families.
